claims.claim: PAS directly governs Kelantan, Terengganu and Kedah, and anchors the PN coalition holding Perlis.
claims.source: Election Commission of Malaysia media statement on gazetted 2023 state-election results, 29 Aug 2023: https://ppn.spr.gov.my/kedah/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/KM-BIL-29-2023-PERWARTAAN-KEPUTUSAN-PRU-DUN-KE-15-DAN-PRK-PARLIMEN-P036-KUALA-TERENGGANU.pdf ; Bernama reporting SPR Perlis result, 20 Nov 2022: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2140460
claims.correction: The claim is broadly supported: PN won Kedah, Kelantan and Terengganu in 2023 and Perlis in 2022; PAS is the dominant party in those PN state governments. For publication, specify election dates and seat counts rather than saying only 'controls'.
claims.stale_check: As of 17 May 2026 I found no later state election changing these governments.

claims.claim: PAS controls Kelantan, Terengganu and Kedah and anchors PN's Perlis state government.
claims.source: Election Commission of Malaysia media statement on gazetted 2023 state-election results, 29 Aug 2023: https://ppn.spr.gov.my/kedah/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/KM-BIL-29-2023-PERWARTAAN-KEPUTUSAN-PRU-DUN-KE-15-DAN-PRK-PARLIMEN-P036-KUALA-TERENGGANU.pdf ; Bernama reporting SPR Perlis result, 20 Nov 2022: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2140460
claims.correction: Use exact seat numbers and distinguish PAS from PN where relevant.
claims.stale_check: No later election was identified before 17 May 2026.

claims.claim: Several state assemblies have tabled Shariah-jurisdiction expansion bills.
claims.source: Bernama, Kelantan assembly motion to re-enact 16 provisions, 6 Mar 2024: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2276742 ; Bernama, Kedah Assembly passes Syariah Court-related bills, 25 Aug 2024: https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2333273 ; Bernama, Terengganu passed Syariah Criminal Offences amendment, 1 Dec 2022: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2144002
claims.correction: The evidence supports Kelantan taking a motion to re-enact provisions annulled by the Federal Court, Kedah passing Syariah Court institutional bills in 2024, and Terengganu passing Syariah criminal-offence amendments in 2022. It is misleading to imply current, parallel 'jurisdiction expansion bills' in Kelantan, Terengganu and Kedah without naming the bills and dates.
claims.stale_check: Some Terengganu action predates the 2024 Nik Elin ruling and is not a recent 2026 development.

claims.claim: Northern state assemblies have tabled bills expanding Shariah court jurisdiction.
claims.source: Bernama, Kedah Assembly passes bill including Syariah Court Enactment Bill 2024, 25 Aug 2024: https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2333273 ; Bernama, Kelantan motion, 6 Mar 2024: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2276742 ; Bernama, Terengganu amendment, 1 Dec 2022: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2144002
claims.correction: Replace with a precise formulation: 'Kelantan moved to re-enact provisions voided by the Federal Court; Kedah passed 2024 Syariah Court-related institutional bills; Terengganu amended its Syariah Criminal Offences enactment in 2022.'
claims.stale_check: The claim appears to compress events from 2022 and 2024 into a single present-tense trend.

claims.claim: The Federal Constitution limits Shariah courts to matters in the State List, primarily personal and family law.
claims.source: Federal Constitution of Malaysia, Ninth Schedule, List II, Item 1, official PDF hosted by Judicial Appointments Commission: https://jac.gov.my/sites/default/files/documents/Federal%20Constitution.pdf
claims.correction: Add the key caveat: State List Item 1 also includes creation and punishment of offences by Muslims against precepts of Islam, except matters in the Federal List, and Syariah courts' criminal jurisdiction is only as conferred by federal law.
claims.stale_check: No recent constitutional amendment identified altering this point.

claims.claim: Proposed expansions in Kelantan, Terengganu and Kedah would touch areas adjacent to federal criminal jurisdiction.
claims.source: Federal Court summary judgment in Nik Elin Zurina Nik Abdul Rashid & Anor v Kerajaan Negeri Kelantan, 9 Feb 2024: https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/sites/default/files/2024-02/Ringkasan%20Penghakiman%20Nik%20Elin.pdf ; Bernama Terengganu amendment report, 1 Dec 2022: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2144002 ; Bernama Kedah bill report, 25 Aug 2024: https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2333273
claims.correction: This is well supported for Kelantan provisions voided in Nik Elin. It is less clearly established for Kedah's 2024 institutional Syariah Court bills, and Terengganu's 2022 criminal-offence amendments should be specified rather than grouped as 'proposed expansions'.
claims.stale_check: No obvious stale correction, but the claim conflates events across different years.

claims.claim: Federal Court rulings in recent years have repeatedly affirmed that civil law prevails where state Shariah enactments overreach the State List.
claims.source: Federal Court summary judgment, Nik Elin, 9 Feb 2024: https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/sites/default/files/2024-02/Ringkasan%20Penghakiman%20Nik%20Elin.pdf ; Federal Court media summary, SIS Forum v Kerajaan Negeri Selangor, 21 Feb 2022: https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/sites/default/files/documents/Ringkasan_Media/2022/PS20220221%5BSIS%5D.pdf ; Chief Justice speech referencing Iki Putra and SIS Forum, 15 Jan 2024: https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/sites/default/files/2024-01/OLY%202024%20-%20SPEECH%20BY%20TRH%20THE%20CHIEF%20JUSTICE%20OF%20MALA_240115_114911.pdf
claims.correction: Use 'constitutional supremacy and federal legislative competence prevail' rather than 'civil law prevails', because the rulings are primarily about constitutional division of legislative powers, not a blanket hierarchy of civil over Syariah law in all matters.

claims.claim: Cases challenging state enactments that touch federal subjects have generally been struck down on constitutional grounds.
claims.source: Federal Court summary judgment, Nik Elin, 9 Feb 2024: https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/sites/default/files/2024-02/Ringkasan%20Penghakiman%20Nik%20Elin.pdf ; Chief Justice speech referencing Iki Putra and SIS Forum, 15 Jan 2024: https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/sites/default/files/2024-01/OLY%202024%20-%20SPEECH%20BY%20TRH%20THE%20CHIEF%20JUSTICE%20OF%20MALA_240115_114911.pdf
claims.correction: The claim should name the cases and avoid 'generally' unless a defined sample is provided. Strong examples include Iki Putra, SIS Forum and Nik Elin.

claims.claim: Malay-Muslim attitudes on Islamic governance are not monolithic.
claims.source: Pew Research Center, Buddhism, Islam and Religious Pluralism in South and Southeast Asia, survey conducted June-Sept 2022, published 12 Sept 2023: https://www.pewresearch.org/2023/09/12/buddhism-islam-and-religious-pluralism-in-south-and-southeast-asia/ ; Merdeka Center/Sisters in Islam Muslim Youth Survey 2022 release: https://sistersinislam.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/Muslim-Youth-Survey-2022-News-Release.pdf
claims.correction: Supported as a broad interpretation of survey variation, but the analysis should cite specific survey questions and populations.
claims.stale_check: No stale issue identified, though survey age and population differences should be disclosed.

claims.claim: The gap between 'Islamic values' and 'full hudud implementation' is wide in survey data.
claims.source: Pew Research Center 2013, The World's Muslims: Religion, Politics and Society: https://www.pewresearch.org/religion/2013/04/30/the-worlds-muslims-religion-politics-society-beliefs-about-sharia/ ; Merdeka Center, Malaysians not ready for hudud, 16 Jul 2014: https://merdeka.org/malaysians-not-ready-for-hudud-merdeka-center/ ; Merdeka Center/Sisters in Islam Muslim Youth Survey 2022 release: https://sistersinislam.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/Muslim-Youth-Survey-2022-News-Release.pdf
claims.correction: This over-simplifies the data. Pew found 86% of Malaysian Muslims favored making sharia official law, while Merdeka's 2014 poll found only 30% of Malay respondents thought Malaysia was ready to implement hudud. But other data, including the 2022 Muslim Youth Survey, shows substantial support for hudud in some forms among Muslim youth. Do not state 'narrower support' without specifying 'readiness now' versus 'support in principle'.
claims.stale_check: The 2014 Merdeka poll may be stale; later youth data can point in a more conservative direction.

claims.claim: Polling consistently shows broad sympathy for Islamic principles in public life and much narrower support for the strict criminal-law version.
claims.source: Pew Research Center 2023 Malaysia survey: https://www.pewresearch.org/2023/09/12/religion-and-politics-2/ ; Merdeka Center/Sisters in Islam Muslim Youth Survey 2022 release: https://sistersinislam.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/Muslim-Youth-Survey-2022-News-Release.pdf ; Merdeka Center 2014 hudud poll: https://merdeka.org/malaysians-not-ready-for-hudud-merdeka-center/
claims.correction: The claim is too strong. Surveys show broad support for sharia or Islamic law, but support for hudud depends heavily on wording, sample and whether the question asks readiness, implementation in principle, or specific punishments. 'Consistently' and 'much narrower' should be softened or replaced with exact figures.
claims.stale_check: Later Muslim youth polling after the 2014 hudud poll weakens the claim if applied to current attitudes.

claims.source: No primary source found directly measuring this binary formulation. Relevant but non-identical sources include Pew 2023: https://www.pewresearch.org/2023/09/12/religion-and-politics-2/ and Merdeka/SIS 2022: https://sistersinislam.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/Muslim-Youth-Survey-2022-News-Release.pdf
claims.correction: This should be framed as analysis or removed unless supported by a poll defining the two 'extremes' and showing a majority rejecting both.

claims.claim: The middle ground has no political champion.
claims.source: No primary source found. This is a political interpretation, not a directly verifiable factual claim.
claims.correction: Label as commentary, or substantiate through party manifestos, speeches and polling about voter representation.

claims.claim: The constitutional middle ground is where most voters sit, and where no party currently camps.
claims.source: No primary source found directly proving both voter location and party-position absence. Relevant survey sources do not directly establish this proposition.
claims.correction: Separate into two claims and support with survey evidence and party-position evidence; otherwise present as opinion.

claims.claim: The constitution already accommodates Islamic law within limits.
claims.source: Federal Constitution of Malaysia, Ninth Schedule, List II, Item 1, official PDF hosted by Judicial Appointments Commission: https://jac.gov.my/sites/default/files/documents/Federal%20Constitution.pdf
claims.correction: Accurate, but should cite the Ninth Schedule, List II, Item 1, and Article 74 on legislative powers.

claims.claim: The real debate is the scope of dual jurisdiction, and who decides where the line falls.
claims.source: Federal Court summary judgment, Nik Elin, 9 Feb 2024: https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/sites/default/files/2024-02/Ringkasan%20Penghakiman%20Nik%20Elin.pdf ; Federal Constitution, Article 74 and Ninth Schedule: https://jac.gov.my/sites/default/files/documents/Federal%20Constitution.pdf
claims.correction: This is interpretive but grounded in constitutional litigation over federal and state legislative competence. For factual precision, say 'a central legal debate' rather than 'the real debate'.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Several state assemblies have tabled Shariah-jurisdiction expansion bills.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The analysis does not identify the bills, dates, states, or legal provisions. Primary/source-adjacent evidence supports different actions in different states and years, not a clean current wave of matching jurisdiction-expansion bills.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Bernama Kelantan 6 Mar 2024 https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2276742 ; Bernama Kedah 25 Aug 2024 https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2333273 ; Bernama Terengganu 1 Dec 2022 https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2144002

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Proposed expansions in Kelantan, Terengganu and Kedah would touch areas adjacent to federal criminal jurisdiction.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: This groups Kelantan provisions voided in Nik Elin, Kedah 2024 institutional court bills, and Terengganu 2022 criminal-offence amendments as if they are the same kind of pending expansion. Kedah's cited bills are not clearly shown to touch federal criminal jurisdiction.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Federal Court Nik Elin summary https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/sites/default/files/2024-02/Ringkasan%20Penghakiman%20Nik%20Elin.pdf ; Bernama Kedah https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2333273

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Polling consistently shows broad sympathy for Islamic principles in public life and much narrower support for the strict criminal-law version.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: Survey findings vary by wording and sample. A 2014 Merdeka poll shows low readiness for hudud, but Pew and 2022 Muslim Youth Survey data show substantial support for sharia or hudud in some forms. 'Consistently' and 'much narrower' overstate the evidence.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Most Malay-Muslims want neither extreme. The middle ground has no political champion.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: No primary source was found that defines the two extremes, measures a majority of Malay-Muslims rejecting both, or proves no party represents the claimed middle ground.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: No direct primary source found; relevant but insufficient surveys include Pew 2023 and SIS/Merdeka 2022.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Federal Court rulings in recent years have repeatedly affirmed that civil law prevails where state Shariah enactments overreach the State List.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The underlying legal point is constitutional supremacy and division of legislative competence, not a blanket rule that 'civil law' always prevails over Syariah law.

omitted_facts: The Federal Court's Nik Elin decision on 9 February 2024 was an 8-1 majority and invalidated 16 of 18 challenged Kelantan Syariah Criminal Code (I) Enactment 2019 provisions; source: Federal Court summary judgment, https://www.kehakiman.gov.my/sites/default/files/2024-02/Ringkasan%20Penghakiman%20Nik%20Elin.pdf

The Chief Justice stated that the Nik Elin petition did not concern the position of Islam or the Syariah courts generally; source: Bernama quoting Chief Justice, 9 Feb 2024, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2269198

The Ninth Schedule State List does not only cover personal and family law; it also allows state law on offences by Muslims against precepts of Islam except matters in the Federal List, with Syariah criminal jurisdiction subject to federal law; source: Federal Constitution PDF, https://jac.gov.my/sites/default/files/documents/Federal%20Constitution.pdf

Act 355 limits Syariah criminal punishment to imprisonment not exceeding three years, fine not exceeding RM5,000 and whipping not exceeding six times; source: Bernama quoting Chief Justice, 9 Feb 2024, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2269198

Kedah's 2024 move included placing the Syariah Court under the state constitution and establishing a Syariah Premier Court, not merely expanding criminal jurisdiction; source: Bernama, 25 Aug 2024, https://bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2333273
source_assessment: The original analysis cites only two T4A related issues and no external primary sources, creating a circular-source problem. It covers legal, governance and social lenses, but the cited-source base has no direct government documents, court rulings, election commission records, official gazettes, survey reports, party documents, civil-society voices, Islamic legal scholars, minority-rights advocates, or state-government justifications. Source diversity is therefore extremely low even though the topic requires legal primary sources and politically diverse perspectives.
overall_assessment: The legal core is directionally right: Malaysia's Constitution limits state Syariah jurisdiction, and recent Federal Court cases have struck down state provisions that intruded into federal competence. However, the analysis is too loose on the state-bill claim, compresses distinct events across Kelantan, Kedah and Terengganu, and overstates survey conclusions about Malay-Muslim opinion. Several political claims are commentary presented as fact. Publication should add exact case names, dates, provisions, election results and survey figures, and should downgrade broad claims about public opinion unless directly sourced.
